coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots synthesized at room temperature for detection of tetracycline hydrochloride
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Yuan
Liu, Jia Hui
Li, Rong Sheng
Li, Yuan Fang
Huang, Cheng Zhi
Zhen, Shu Jun
coredata.dc:description: The traditional synthesis methods of carbon dots (CDs) have some disadvantages of complicated operation and a large amount of energy consumption. To address these limitations, we synthesized yellow-green luminescent CDs at room temperature according to the principle of amine-aldehyde condensation in this work. This reaction is simple, economical, energy saving and is extremely consistent with the concept of green synthesis and sustainable development. In addition, studies have found that tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) can quench the fluorescence of the as-prepared CDs through Inner filter effect (IFE). The changes of the fluorescence intensity also have a good linear relationship with the concentration of TC in the range of 10.0–400.0 μM, and the detection limit is 6.0 μM. This method has been successfully used to determine the tetracycline content of tetracycline tablets. Finally, the interaction mechanism between TC and CDs was analyzed and discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature synthesis
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of the selectivity of TC detection
Detection of TC content in TC tablets
Morphology and structure characterization of CDs
Optical properties of the as-prepared CDs
Research and discussion of TC detection mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of the CDs. A, TEM imaging; B, particle size distribution; C, AFM imaging; D, a sectional view drawn along the height of the white line in (C).

The elemental composition and functional group analysis of CDs. A, FT-IR of o-phenylenediamine, glyoxal and CDs; B, Raman spectra of CDs; C, XPS survey spectrum of CDs; D, High-resolution of C1s spectra.

The optical properties of CDs. A, the blue curve corresponds to the UV absorption spectrum of CDs, and the black and red curves represent the maximum excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of CDs, respectively; the inset optical images underdaylight (left) andunder the 365 nm UV light lamp (right); B, FL emission spectra of CDs under the different excitation wavelengths vary from 360 nm to 460 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Detection of TC. A, the Fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases with increasing of TC concentration (0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 130, 160, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 μM); B, linear relationship between F0/F and various TC concentrations; C, the effect of various antibiotics on the fluorescence intensities of CDs (The concentrations of antibiotics were 400 μM); D, linear relationship between HPLC peak area and tetracycline concentration (0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7 g/L).

The mechanisms of TC detection by CDs. A, UV spectrum of TC (blue), and the maximum excitation (red) and emission (black) fluorescence spectra of CDs; B, fluorescent lifetime of CDs before and after adding 100 μM TC; C, cyclic voltammetry curve of the CDs in the solution state; D, electron transfer between CDs and TC. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
A schematic diagram of the synthesis of CDs from o-phenylenediamine and glyoxal at room temperature for highly selective detection of TC.
Effect of various other excipients in TC tablets on TC detection.

row.entry: c TC, 300 μM; pH 4.0; λ = 380 nm.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots synthesized at room temperature for detection of tetracycline hydrochloride

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analysis, Chongqing Science & Technology Commission, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analysis

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The traditional synthesis methods of carbon dots (CDs) have some disadvantages of complicated operation and a large amount of energy consumption. To address these limitations, we synthesized yellow-green luminescent CDs at room temperature according to the principle of amine-aldehyde condensation in this work. This reaction is simple, economical, energy saving and is extremely consistent with the concept of green synthesis and sustainable development. In addition, studies have found that tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) can quench the fluorescence of the as-prepared CDs through Inner filter effect (IFE). The changes of the fluorescence intensity also have a good linear relationship with the concentration of TC in the range of 10.0–400.0 μM, and the detection limit is 6.0 μM. This method has been successfully used to determine the tetracycline content of tetracycline tablets. Finally, the interaction mechanism between TC and CDs was analyzed and discussed.

Carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized at room temperature with a spherical structure. Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) can quench the fluorescence of the as-prepared CDs through Inner filter effect (IFE) because the ultraviolet absorption band of TC overlaps with the excitation band of CDs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: CDs are a new class of emerged fluorescent nanomaterial with many excellent properties such as adjustable photoluminescence, good water solubility, low toxicity, low cost, light stability and chemical stability [1]. Therefore, they have been widely used in analytical detection [2], nanomedicine [3], electricity or photocatalysis [4,5], bio-imaging [6], and some other fields. After more than a decade of development, many different types of CDs have been discovered and synthesized. According to their properties, crystal structures and quantum confinement effects, CDs can be classified into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [7], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [8], carbon nanodots (CNDs) [9] and polymer dots (PDs) [10]. The traditional synthesis methods of CDs can be classified into “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches according to the relationships between sources and products. The “top-down” method refers to destroying or broking down the carbon material of the macromolecular structure into small-sized carbon nanoparticles through certain physical or chemical ways, including electrochemical etching, acid etching, arc discharge, high-temperature pyrolysis and laser ablation methods [11]. The “bottom-up” method mentions to the synthesis or agglomeration of small-molecule organic carbon materials into larger molecular weight carbon nanoparticles by certain chemical methods including direct carbonization method, microwave-assisted synthesis method, hydrothermal method, solvent heat method and other methods [12]. Microwave-assisted synthesis method takes only a few minutes and hydrothermal method is easy to operate. Although these synthesis approaches are fast and simple, they are always needing cumbersome experimental instruments and many of them are very energy-consuming. So synthesizing CDs at room temperature without the need for external energy is more ideal and practical. Generally, there are two frequently used strategies to synthesize CDs at room temperature. One is synthesizing CDs under the action of strong oxidants such as concentrated sulfuric acid, nitric acid [13] and H2O2 [14] through oxidation reaction, and the other one is the dehydration and condensation reactions of reactants under the conditions of strong alkali such as concentrated sodium hydroxide or potassium hydroxide [15]. Our group has also synthesized CDs without strong acid or alkali in recent years [16]. However, the synthesis time of these methods generally takes more than one day, which is time-consuming. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple, green and time-saving method to synthesize CDs.

Tetracycline (TC) is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug discovered in the middle of the 20th century [17]. It can be used to treat human and animal diseases owing to its capacity to against gram-positive bacteria and negative bacteria, such as rickettsia, mycoplasma, large viruses, spirochetes, chlamydia and some protozoa [18]. In addition, TC also has the advantages of low cost and good oral absorption. So it has been widely used in animal husbandry. However, such excessive TC applications can bring serious effects to human health because TC residues might be accumulated in food like meat and milk. It has been found that intaking TC frequently will cause human liver damage and kidney harm. And pregnancy women are more sensitive to TC hepatotoxicity. Last but not least, lots of data has shown that the long-time and repeated using of TC is harmful to teeth because it can affect the growth and formation of teeth and make the color of teeth change to yellow. Capillary electrophoresis (CE) [19], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [20], spectrophotometric method and chemo-luminescence (CL) [21] are commonly used to detect TC. But these methods always have the disadvantages of time-consuming, expensive instruments and tedious operation. So It is very important to establish a simple and fast way to detect TC selectively and accurately. Although Miao [22] and Li [23] et al. have developed fluorescent method to monitor TC quickly and selectively, but the synthesis of their probes have shortcomings of energy consumption and high cost.

Herein, we synthesized a yellow-green fluorescent CDs according to the principle of the amine-aldehyde condensation reaction to produce Schiff base by using glyoxal as carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen source at the room temperature for 3h (Scheme 1 ). The CDs was synthesized by mixing glyoxal and o-phenylenediamine without adding any other substances. Because TC's ultraviolet absorption partially overlaps with the excitation band or emission band of the CDs, TC could quench the fluorescence of CDs through Inner filter effect (IFE). Thus, TC could be detected sensitively and selectively based on the obvious quenched fluorescence of CDs.

Penicillin G sodium salt (PenG), glyoxal, dextrin, o-phenylenediamine, cefalotin sodium, dl-penicillamine (DL-pen) and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tetracycline Tablets (qualification: 0.25 g) was bought in Yifeng Grand Pharmacy Chain Co. Ltd; Streptomycin sulfate (STR), kanamycin sulfate (KAN) was purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). All chemicals were analytical reagents. Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer was employed to control the acidity. Water used in the experiment is ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, Mili-Q, Germany).

F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) was used to measure the fluorescence spectra of CDs. Shimadzu UV-3600 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to detect the characteristic absorption spectrum of the CDs and TC. Tecnai G2 F20 Field-emission transmission electron microscope (FEI company, America) was used to obtain CDs' appearance image. ESCALAB-250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was used to analyse the elemental composition of CDs surface. Hitachi FTIR-8400S fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) was used to measure the infrared spectrum of the CDs. FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrometer was used to determine the fluorescence life time. (LC-2130 type) High Performance Liquid Chromatograph(HPLC) was applied for measuring TC content of the TC tablets. LabRAM HR800 laser confocal Raman spectrometer was used to detect CDs’ Raman spectrum.

CDs were synthesized at room temperature without consuming energy. At first, 0.05 g/mL o-phenylenediamine solution was prepared by dissolving 0.2 g accurately weighed o-phenylenediamine in 4 mL water under ultrasound (under low temperature conditions). Then two reaction reagents according to the volume ratio of o-phenylenediamine (0.05 g/mL): glyoxal (40%) = 1:6 were added into 50 mL small beaker and mixed. After reacting at room temperature for 3 h, the yellow-green fluorescence could be observed under the UV light lamp and then CDs crude product was gained. Approximately equal volume of 1 M NaOH was used to adjust an alkaline pH (pH ≈ 11, until there is no longer a yellowish precipitate) to stop the reaction. Finally, CDs crude product was purified by centrifugation. The suspension was centrifugated for 10 min using 12000 rpm to remove supernatant with impurities like small organic molecules. The precipitation was washed by pure water three times. Last, 0.1 M HCl was used to dissolve the yellow precipitation and the as-prepared CDs solution was kept in the refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

700 μL CDs which was diluted 1000 times and 100 μL TC with different concentrations were mixed and diluted to 1.0 mL with BR buffer (pH 4.0). The fluorescence intensity of the mixture was detected by using F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Under the excitation of the 380 nm and the voltage of the 700 V, the fluorescence emissions were measured between 400 nm and 700 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the selectivity of TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: 700 μL diluted carbon dots solution and 200 μL BR buffer (pH = 4.0) were mixed together. Then 100 μL 4 mM TC, PenG, KAN, STR, cefalotin and DL-pen were added into the mixture, respectively. Finally, the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 500 nm was measured under the same conditions.

600 μL diluted carbon dots solution, 200 μL BR buffer (pH 4.0), 100 μL 3 mM TC were mixed, and then 100 μL water, starch, talcum powder, dextrin, glucose, Magnesium stearate with different concentrations were added, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC content in TC tablets
ce:section.ce:para: TC tablets (specifications: 0.25 g) were purchased from Yifeng Grand Pharmacy Chain Co., Ltd. The process of treating sample was as follow: five TC tablets were taken at random and accurately weighed at 2.5747 g. The mixture was placed into a mortar and ground together to a fine powder. A certain amount of TC drug powder was weighed into a 5 mL EP tube and the mass was recorded. Then 4 mL of 1 M HCl was added to dissolve the TC powder under ultrasonic condition. In a 1.5 mL EP tube, 700 μL of diluted CDs solution, 200 μL of BR buffer solution at pH 4.0, 100 μL of TC solution at different concentrations were sequentially added and the solution was mixed and measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The yellow-green luminous CDs were synthesized at room temperature. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 A) indicated that the as-prepared CDs were spherical and had good dispersion. At the same time, CDs had an average size of 2.75 nm at the range from 1.5 nm to 4.5 nm (Fig. 1B), which was calculated by analyzing the average size of 100 single CDs (Nano Measurer 1.2 software). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurement result (Fig. 1C) showed that CDs’ height was lower than 2.0 nm (Fig. 1D).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to characterize the surface groups and structures of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, o-phenylenediamine exhibits strong characteristic absorption of the NH bending vibration at 1632 cm−1 [24] and glyoxal has the peak of 1635 cm−1 owing to the stretching vibration of the CO [25]. The as-prepared CDs have four strong absorption peaks which are attributed to OH and NH stretching vibrations at 3416 cm−1, the COO- stretching vibration at 1461 cm−1 [26], the CO stretching vibration at 1073 cm−1 and the CN stretching vibration at 1655 cm−1. The existence of CN bond reveals that the formation mechanism of CDs is through the amine-aldehyde condensation reaction of amino groups with aldehyde groups to form Schiff base [27]. The Raman spectroscopy shows two main peaks at 1360 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1 (Fig. 2B), which are D band and G band partly resulting from the sp3 drawback and the in-plane vibration of the sp2 C [28]. So there was amide, hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl existing in the as-prepared CDs. The X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to make sure the elemental composition and functional group of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, XPS full survey spectrum of the obtained CDs presents three peaks at 284.8, 399.55 and 532.17 eV, which corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s [29] respectively. These results show that the as-prepared CDs mainly contain carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. In the high resolution of the C1s region, there are four peaks (Fig. 2D), indicating that carbon is present in four different chemical environments corresponding to CC/CC at 284.3 eV, sp3 C from CO at 285.2 eV, CCN at 286.8 eV and CO/CN at 288 eV. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S1, ESI†) shows that CDs have four peaks, which are assigned to NH at 400.8 eV, N-(C)3 at 399.9 eV, CNC at 399.2 eV and CN at 398.4 eV [30]. In addition, there are three peaks at 532.6 eV, 532 eV and 531.2 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. S2, ESI†), which are attributed to the OCO*、*OCO and OH bands, respectively. The existence of CN bond further demonstrates that the formation mechanism of CDs is caused by amine-aldehyde condensation reaction to form Schiff base. These results also indicate that CDs contain some active reactive groups such as amino and hydroxyl functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the as-prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The remarkable properties of the as-obtained CDs were confirmed by the optical spectra. The UV–visible spectra (Fig. 3 A) shows absorption peaks at 274.5 nm, 305.1 nm and 372 nm. The first two are ascribed to the wide UV–visible absorption bands due to π-π* transitions [31] and the last one explains that CDs has an UV–visible absorption band caused by the n-π* transition from CO [32]. In addition, the maximum excitation wavelength of as-prepared CDs is 430 nm and the maximum emission wavelength is 500 nm. CDs exhibit strong yellow-green luminescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light lamp. The excitation-dependent emission phenomenon was observed when excited from 360 nm to 500 nm (Fig. 3B), which possibly corresponds to the distribution of different sizes and distinctive surface states.

Next, we investigate the stability of CDs. The pH has a great influence on CDs. The experimental result (Fig. S3, ESI†) shows that CDs has stronger fluorescence under the conditions of the acidity (BR pH = 1.88) and alkaline (BR pH = 12.04). With the pH increasing, CDs' fluorescence intensity decreased firstly and then increased gradually. And the fluorescence intensity was lowest when the pH was neutral (BR pH = 6.99). This may be attributed to the abundant hydroxyl group on the surface of the CDs. Under acidic conditions, OH on the surface of CDs can form hydrogen bond with H+ to make the system more stable. Under alkaline conditions, the interaction between COOH on the surface of CDs and OH− also can make the carboxylate groups on the surface of the CDs get deprotonated [33], and the electrostatic repulsion makes the CDs dispersed. The CDs can be better dispersed in these two situations. Therefore, the CDs have stronger fluorescence intensity under acidic and alkaline conditions. The as-prepared CDs have good salt stability because its fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged when the concentration of sodium chloride increased from 0 to 1.0 mM (Fig. S4, ESI†). After irradiating for 10 min under UV light, CDs’ fluorescence intensity is reduced substantially with the irradiation time (Fig. S5, ESI†).

The as-prepared CDs can be used for detecting TC. As shown in Fig. 4 A, as the concentration of TC increased, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased regularly. The changes of the fluorescence intensity (F
0
/F, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of TC, respectively) have a good linear relationship with the concentration of TC in the range of 10.0 μM–400.0 μM (Fig. 4B). The linear equation is F
0
/F = 0.00619c TC+0.9234, and the correlation coefficient is R
2 = 0.9966. The detection limit is 6.0 μM (3σ/k). Compared with previous fluorescence detection methods, this method has a wide detection range [34]. The decreased intensity of CDs caused by TC is significantly greater than that of other antibiotics (Fig. 4C), indicating that this method is selective for TC detection.

Then we investigated the interference of other substances in the TC tablets on the determination of TC. The experimental results showed that excipient starch, talcum powder, dextrin, glucose and magnesium stearate in the TC tablets had no effect on the determination of TC because the absolute value of fluorescence change ratios were lower than 10% (Table 1 ). Finally, the synthesized CDs were used to determine the TC content in TC tablets. The experimental results showed that each tetracycline tablet contained 0.2480 g (n = 5, RSD = 2.6%) of TC on average. In order to verify the feasibility of this method, the content of TC in the same sample of TC tablets was determined by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). First, a standard concentration of TC solution was prepared to draw a standard curve (Fig. 4D). Then, the TC tablets were tested to have a TC content of 0.2445 g (Table S1, ESI†), which was consistent with the specifications of the TC tablets and further verified the accuracy of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research and discussion of TC detection mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
We further explored the mechanism of TC detection. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the ultraviolet absorption band of TC overlaps with the excitation band of CDs, indicating that the fluorescence of CDs may be quenched by TC through inner filter effect (IFE). Then we investigated whether the quenching process is dynamic quenching or static quenching. The fluorescence lifetime of the as-prepared CDs was 6.38 ns. It hardly changed after adding 100 μM TC (Fig. 5B). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching process of TC on CDs is static quenching and its detection mechanism is IFE. In addition, in order to verify the presence or absence of electron transfer, we calculated the highest occupied molecular orbital (HUMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital energy level (LUMO) of the CDs using the following empirical formula [35]:
EHUMO = -e (EOX+4.4)
ELUMO = -e (Ered+4.4)

First, the cyclic voltammetry curve of the CDs was determined in the solution state. Eox represents the oxidation potential of CDs. As shown in Fig. 5C, the reduction potential Ered of CDs is −0.18 eV and then ELUMO = −4.22 eV can be calculated according to the formula. Finally, we calculate the energy level EHUMO by combining Eg and Ered [36]:
EHUMO = ELUMO-Eg

The optical bandgap Eg was calculated from the absorption edge of the CDs absorption spectrum as 5.48 eV (Fig. S6, ESI†), so the EHUMO value is calculated to be −9.7 eV [37]. According to the B3LYP method in Dmol3 mode, the EHOMO and ELUMO of TC can be calculated to be −6.2 eV and −3.87 eV, respectively. CDs can be excited after absorbing the light. Since the LUMO orbital energy level of TC is higher than that of CDs, The photo-excited electrons of the CDs cannot be transferred to the LUMO of TC [38] (Fig. 5D). Therefore, photo-induced electron transfer (PET) cannot occur between carbon dots and tetracycline.

Finally, the infrared spectra of CDs before and after the addition of TC were measured and compared. The experimental results (Fig. S7, ESI†) showed that after the addition of TC, the absorption peaks of CDs had no obvious change. There was no significant red-shift or blue-shift at 3416 cm−1, 1655 cm−1, and 1073 cm−1 after the addition of TC. In addition, the addition of TC did not generate new infrared absorption peaks, indicating that no new functional groups were generated. In summary, the mechanism of TC detection is IFE.

In summary, we have successfully prepared the yellow-green photo-luminescent CDs at room temperature by using a one-step method according to the amine-aldehyde condensation reaction and applied them to the detection of TC through the IFE mechanism. There are at least two advantages of our method. First, the preparation method of CDs is simple, quick, cost-effective and economical energy saving. Second, the detection method of TC by the as-prepared CDs is sensitive and selective, which has been successfully applied in the detection of TC in TC tablets, showing great potential in the pharmaceutical analysis area.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 21535006), and the fund of Chongqing Fundamental and Advanced Research Project (cstc2017jcyjBX0068).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2019.02.047.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent and non-toxic carbon dots using Stevia rebaudiana Bertoni

Full-color emission polymer carbon dots with quench-resistant solid-state fluorescence
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Carbon dots: materials, synthesis, properties and approaches to long-wavelength and multicolor emission
Controlled synthesis of green and blue luminescent carbon nanoparticles with high yields by the carbonization of sucrose
A large-scale synthesis of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots: a self-exothermic reaction driving the formation of the nanocrystalline core at room temperature
Room temperature and solvothermal green synthesis of self passivated carbon quantum dots
Large-scale simultaneous synthesis of highly photoluminescent green amorphous carbon nanodots and yellow crystalline graphene quantum dots at room temperature
Trace determination of tetracyclines in water samples by capillary zone electrophoresis combining off-line and on-line sample preconcentration
Determination of tetracycline hydrochloride by terahertz spectroscopy with PLSR model
Determination of tetracycline residues in bovine milk, serum, and urine by capillary electrophoresis
Determination of residues of tetracycline antibiotics in animal tissues by high-performance liquid chromatography
Molecularly imprinted on-line solid-phase extraction combined with flow-injection chemiluminescence for the determination of tetracycline
Carbon dots derived from tobacco for visually distinguishing and detecting three kinds of tetracyclines

On–Off–on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
BiOBr/BiOCl/carbon quantum dot microspheres with superior visible light-driven photocatalysis
Pyridoxine and pyridoxal analogs. V. Syntheses and infrared spectra of Schiff bases
Carbon dots enhance the stability of CdS for visible-light-driven overall water splitting
Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism
An active structure preservation method for developing functional graphitic carbon dots as an effective antibacterial agent and a sensitive pH and Al(iii) nanosensor
Carbon dots based FRET for the detection of DNA damage
A surfactant-assisted redox hydrothermal route to prepare highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots with aggregation-induced emission enhancement properties
Highly selective detection of phosphate in very complicated matrixes with an off–on fluorescent probe of europium-adjusted carbon dots
An inner filter effect based sensor of tetracycline hydrochloride as developed by loading photoluminescent carbon nanodots in the electrospun nanofibers
Solid-Phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
Branched polyethylenimine-functionalized carbon dots as sensitive and selective fluorescent probes for N-acetylcysteine via an off-on mechanism
Synthesis of nitrogen-doping carbon dots with different photoluminescence properties by controlling the surface states
Inner filter with carbon quantum dots: a selective sensing platform for detection of hematin in human red cells
